questions.question: Two consumers have marginal benefits for a public good: MB1 = 80 - 2Q and MB2 = 40 - Q. Marginal cost is MC = 60. What is the efficient quantity?
questions.explanation: Samuelson condition: MB1 + MB2 = MC. Thus 120 - 3Q = 60, so Q = 20. A uses only consumer 1's MB condition. C uses only consumer 2's MB condition incorrectly. D horizontally sums as if it were a private good.

questions.question: Three households value flood protection with marginal benefits 50 - Q, 40 - Q, and 30 - Q. The marginal cost is MC = 60. What is the efficient level of flood protection?
questions.explanation: Sum marginal benefits: MSB = 120 - 3Q. Set 120 - 3Q = 60, so Q = 20. B uses two households' benefits. C ignores the negative slope. D uses only the highest individual benefit curve.

questions.question: For a public good, MSB = 150 - 3Q and MC = c. What happens to efficient Q when MC falls from 60 to 30?

choices.A: It falls from 30 to 40 because lower cost reduces scarcity.
choices.B: It rises from 30 to 40 because the lower MC line intersects MSB at a higher quantity.
choices.C: It stays at 50 because MSB is unchanged.
choices.D: It rises from 60 to 90 because MC is subtracted from each individual's MB.

questions.explanation: Efficiency sets 150 - 3Q = c. At c = 60, Q = 30; at c = 30, Q = 40. The mechanism is a downward shift in marginal cost. A reverses the direction. C ignores the changed cost. D uses an incorrect formula.

questions.question: A town's efficient public good provision satisfies MB1 + MB2 = MC. Initially MB1 = 60 - Q and MB2 = 40 - Q. MC = 50. If a third resident with MB3 = 30 - Q moves in, what happens to efficient Q?

choices.A: It rises from 25 to 26.67 because the vertical sum of marginal benefits increases.
choices.B: It falls from 25 to 20 because more people increase free riding.
choices.C: It stays at 25 because MC is unchanged.
choices.D: It rises from 50 to 80 because quantities are horizontally summed.

questions.explanation: Initially, 100 - 2Q = 50, so Q = 25. With the third resident, 130 - 3Q = 50, so Q = 26.67. The mechanism is an upward shift in summed marginal benefits. B confuses efficient provision with voluntary provision. C ignores the new beneficiary. D uses private-good summation.

questions.question: On a public good graph, individual MB curves are vertically summed into an MSB curve. The efficient quantity is where MSB crosses MC. What must be true at that point?

choices.A: Each individual's marginal benefit equals marginal cost.
choices.B: The sum of individuals' marginal benefits equals marginal cost.
choices.C: The average of individuals' marginal benefits equals marginal cost.

questions.explanation: The Samuelson condition is sum of marginal benefits equals marginal cost. A is the private good condition incorrectly applied to each person. C undercounts benefits. D is unrelated.

questions.question: A graph compares efficient provision of a private good and a public good. Which interpretation is correct?

choices.A: For a private good, demands are vertically summed; for a public good, demands are horizontally summed.
choices.B: For a private good, marginal rates of substitution are summed across people; for a public good, each person's MRS must equal MC separately.
choices.C: For a private good, quantities are horizontally summed at a common price; for a public good, willingnesses to pay are vertically summed at a common quantity.
choices.D: Both goods use the same aggregation rule because all demand curves slope downward.

questions.explanation: Private goods are rival, so people consume different quantities at the same price; public goods are jointly consumed, so marginal willingnesses to pay are summed for the same quantity. A reverses the rules. B reverses the efficiency conditions. D ignores rivalry.

questions.question: What is the economic intuition behind the Samuelson condition for efficient public good provision?

choices.A: Because everyone consumes the same unit, society should add all individuals' marginal willingnesses to pay for that unit and compare the sum to marginal cost.
choices.B: Because public goods are free to produce, marginal cost should be ignored.
choices.C: Because only the highest-value consumer matters for efficiency.
choices.D: Because each person must buy a separate unit of the public good.

questions.explanation: A unit of a public good benefits all consumers simultaneously, so the relevant marginal benefit is the sum across consumers. B falsely assumes zero cost. C ignores other beneficiaries. D describes private goods.

questions.question: A county is deciding how much mosquito control to provide. All residents benefit from the same spraying level, and one resident's protection does not reduce another's. Which efficiency rule should the county use?

choices.A: Provide spraying until each resident's marginal benefit separately equals marginal cost.
choices.B: Provide spraying until the sum of residents' marginal benefits equals marginal cost.
choices.C: Provide spraying until the lowest marginal benefit equals zero.
choices.D: Provide spraying only if one resident is willing to pay the full marginal cost.

questions.explanation: Mosquito control is a public good over the protected area, so the Samuelson condition applies. A applies the private-good condition incorrectly. C is not an efficiency rule. D ignores aggregate benefits.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes the public good efficiency condition from the private good efficiency condition?

choices.A: Public good efficiency requires summing marginal benefits across consumers; private good efficiency requires each consumer's marginal benefit for their own units to equal price or marginal cost.
choices.B: Public good efficiency requires each person to consume a different quantity; private good efficiency requires everyone consume the same quantity.
choices.C: Public good efficiency ignores marginal cost; private good efficiency ignores marginal benefit.
choices.D: Public good efficiency uses horizontal summation; private good efficiency uses vertical summation.

questions.explanation: Public goods are jointly consumed, so marginal benefits are vertically summed. Private goods are rival, so efficiency allocates units so each consumer's marginal benefit equals marginal cost. B reverses consumption patterns. C is false. D reverses aggregation rules.

questions.question: Two residents value a public good with MB1 = 70 - Q and MB2 = 50 - Q. MC = 40. If the market provides Q = 20 but the efficient quantity is Q = 40, what is the deadweight loss from underprovision?
questions.explanation: MSB = 120 - 2Q. At Q = 20, MSB = 80; at Q = 40, MSB = 40 = MC. DWL is 0.5 × 20 × 40 = 400. A halves the triangle. C doubles it. D uses total benefit over the missing units without subtracting cost.
